footnotes.text: Anathema . . . from Christ: i.e., to be eternally separated from Christ. So great was St. Paul's longing for the salvation of his own kinsmen that he would make any possible sacrifice to that end, even to being separated from Christ, if it were permissible to entertain such a desire. That these words are merely an emphatic way of declaring his great devotion to his people and that they are not to be taken literally is evident from what St. Paul has said above, 8, 38. 6-12: St. Paul here teaches that the true Israelite is not merely the one who is physically descended from Abraham, but the one who has been the recipient of the divine promises, which God lavishes on whom He will, independently of personal merit. --- 7: Gen. 21, 12.
The hatred towards Esau here attributed to God may be explained in the sense of "loving less," in accord with a common Hebrew idiom.

The question here under consideration is the call to the faith, and not predestination to glory. The efficacious call to the faith is a pure gratuity on God's part; man cannot merit it. No one deserves it for "all have sinned and have need of the glory of God." Cf. 3, 23.
Not of him who wills: the primary and ultimate factor in man's destiny is the activity of God's grace, which of course does not exclude man's co-operation.

For this very purpose: it is not to be understood that God's primary and express purpose in creating Pharaoh was to make a sinner out of him. But God raised him up to rule the Egyptian people, and, foreseeing that Pharaoh would abuse grace and fall into sin, God decreed to use Pharaoh according to his demerits for the further manifestation of His own divine attributes and for the realization of the designs of His all-wise providence.
He hardens: i.e., by withdrawing divine grace in punishment of demerits.

St. John Chrysostom says, "St. Paul here so speaks not by way of denying free will but to show to what extent we are to submit to God. For we should be no more ready to demand reasons from God than the clay vessel" (Hom. 16 on Romans).
